evidence_text: SWE-agent reports pass@1 of 12.5% on SWE-bench and 87.7% on HumanEvalFix.
source_excerpt: The abstract states that SWE-agent was evaluated on SWE-bench and HumanEvalFix, "achieving state-of-the-art performance on both with a pass@1 rate of 12.5% and 87.7%, respectively."
note: These are headline benchmark results reported in the abstract.
caveat: These are headline benchmark results reported in the abstract.

evidence_text: With GPT-4 Turbo, SWE-agent solved 12.47% of 2,294 SWE-bench test tasks, compared with a previous best resolve rate of 3.8% from a non-interactive retrieval-augmented system.
source_excerpt: Section 1 states: "Using GPT-4 Turbo as a base LM, SWE-agent solves 12.47% of the 2,294 SWE-bench test tasks, substantially outperforming the previous best resolve rate of 3.8% by a non-interactive, retrieval-augmented system."
note: This is a direct benchmark comparison against the cited prior non-interactive baseline.
caveat: This is a direct benchmark comparison against the cited prior non-interactive baseline.

evidence_text: On SWE-bench Lite, GPT-4 Turbo scored 18.00% with SWE-agent, 11.00% with the Shell-only agent, and 2.67% with RAG.
source_excerpt: Table 1 lists SWE-bench Lite results for GPT-4 Turbo as: RAG 2.67%, Shell-only agent 11.00%, and SWE-agent 18.00%.
note: This evidence isolates the Lite-split comparison across the three evaluated interface settings for the same base model.
caveat: This evidence isolates the Lite-split comparison across the three evaluated interface settings for the same base model.

evidence_text: The ACI transferred to Claude 3 Opus, where SWE-agent solved 10.46% of full SWE-bench tasks and 13.00% of SWE-bench Lite tasks.
source_excerpt: Section 1 says, "Although our ACI was developed for GPT-4 Turbo, we show that it is portable to a different LM; SWE-agent with Claude 3 Opus can solve 10.5% of the benchmark tasks." Table 1 reports Claude 3 Opus at 10.46% on SWE-bench and 13.00% on SWE-bench Lite.
note: The text frames this as portability of the ACI to a different language model.
caveat: The text frames this as portability of the ACI to a different language model.

evidence_text: The paper claims LM-tailored ACIs improve software-engineering performance by providing a small action set, guardrails, and concise command feedback instead of relying only on the default Linux shell.
source_excerpt: The paper says SWE-agent's ACI "offers a small set of simple actions for viewing, searching through and editing files," "uses guardrails to prevent common mistakes," and gives "specific, concise feedback about a command's effects at every turn." It also says ACIs tailored for LMs outperform UIs designed for human users such as the Linux shell.
note: This evidence states the interface features the paper presents as the operative design changes.
caveat: This evidence states the interface features the paper presents as the operative design changes.

evidence_text: SWE-agent adds find_file, search_file, and search_dir commands, and each search returns at most 50 results; if a query exceeds that limit, the agent is told to use a more specific query.
citation: Yang et al., 2024, §3 Search and navigation
source_excerpt: In Section 3, the authors introduce "the special commands find_file, search_file, and search_dir" and state that "The search commands return at most 50 results for each search query; if a search exceeds this number, we do not report the results and instead suggest that the agent write a more specific query."
note: This is an implementation detail of the search interface.
caveat: This is an implementation detail of the search interface.

evidence_text: The file viewer shows at most 100 lines at a time, and the edit command replaces a specified line range and then automatically shows the updated file content.
citation: Yang et al., 2024, §3 File viewer and File editor
source_excerpt: Section 3 says, "The file viewer presents a window of at most 100 lines of the file at a time." It also says the edit command "takes 3 required arguments: the start line, end line, and replacement text" and that "After edits are applied, the file viewer automatically displays the updated content."
note: This evidence describes the file-view and edit workflow exposed to the agent.
caveat: This evidence describes the file-view and edit workflow exposed to the agent.

evidence_text: The edit function runs a linter, shows selected error feedback if a syntax problem is introduced, discards the invalid edit, and asks the agent to try again.
source_excerpt: The paper states, "we integrate a code linter into the edit function to alert the agent of mistakes it may have introduced," that "Select errors from the linter are shown to the agent," and that "Invalid edits are discarded, and the agent is asked to try editing the file again."
note: This is a guardrail in the editing interface rather than a benchmark outcome.
caveat: This is a guardrail in the editing interface rather than a benchmark outcome.

evidence_text: On SWE-bench Lite, summarized search scored 18.0%, iterative search scored 12.0%, and no-search scored 15.7%.
source_excerpt: Table 3 reports Lite performance for the Search ablation as: "Summarized 18.0," "Iterative 12.0," and "No search 15.7."
note: These ablation results compare search-interface variants within the SWE-agent setup.
caveat: These ablation results compare search-interface variants within the SWE-agent setup.

evidence_text: On SWE-bench Lite, editing with linting scored 18.0%, editing without linting scored 15.0%, and the no-edit setting scored 10.3%.
source_excerpt: Table 3 lists Editor ablations as "edit action 15.0 ↓3.0," "w/ linting 18.0," and "Noedit 10.3 ↓7.7." Section 5.1 states, "This intervention improves performance considerably (without linting, 15.0%↓3.0)."
note: This evidence ties a specific editing guardrail to measured performance differences.
caveat: This evidence ties a specific editing guardrail to measured performance differences.

evidence_text: Editing remained a persistent failure point: 1,185 of 2,294 GPT-4 Turbo trajectories (51.7%) had at least one failed edit, and the probability of eventual success dropped from 90.5% for any edit attempt to 57.2% after one failed edit.
source_excerpt: Section 5.2 states, "out of 2,294 task instances, 1,185 (51.7%) of SWE-agent w/ GPT-4 Turbo trajectories have 1+ failed edits." It also says, "Any attempt at editing has a 90.5% chance of eventually being successful. This probability drops off to 57.2% after a single failed edit."
note: This evidence shows a limitation in the agent's edit-recovery behavior even with guardrails.
caveat: This evidence shows a limitation in the agent's edit-recovery behavior even with guardrails.

evidence_text: Resolved runs tended to finish earlier and cheaper: successful GPT-4 runs had a median cost of $1.21 and 12 steps, while unsuccessful ones had a mean cost of $2.52 and 21 steps.
source_excerpt: The abstract states that SWE-agent was evaluated on SWE-bench and HumanEvalFix, "achieving state-of-the-art performance on both with a pass@1 rate of 12.5% and 87.7%, respectively."
note: These are headline benchmark results reported in the abstract.
caveat: These are headline benchmark results reported in the abstract.
